description: EXPORT-kind conformance corpus for @agledger/verify-core verifyAuditExport. Each vector is a REAL /audit-export document produced by the agledger-api engine, then tamper-transformed; pass/fail + canonical FailureCode are asserted by the corpus runner.

vectors.note: Clean 3-entry signed chain verified against out-of-band keys.
Entry 2 row chainPosition renumbered to 5 — monotonic position broken; after sort the gap surfaces at the position-3 entry.
Genesis entry carries a non-null previousHash — chain head removed.
Entry 2 previousHash no longer matches entry 1 payloadHash.
Entry 2 is missing the coseSign1 field.
Entry 2 coseSign1 is non-COSE bytes (hash re-stamped so decode is what fails).
Entry 2 has a valid signature but its signed -65537 chain claim (prevHash) disagrees with the chain — the visible columns were renumbered after signing.
Entry 2 signature byte flipped; envelope still decodes and the chain claim matches, so signature verification is what fails.
Entry 2 references signingKeyId=ghost-key with no key embedded or out of band.
canonicalization JCS (not RFC8949-CDE) — precedes any chain walk.
Zero entries — a chain with nothing to verify is never a silent pass.
Clean chain, but the caller requires a key id no entry uses — policy violation at the first entry.

Entry 2 re-signed with the attacker key (identical predicate, pure key swap) and embedded in signingPublicKeys. With no options it PASSES — documenting that embedded keys are trusted by default. The requireOutOfBandKeys vector below is the same fixture failing closed.

Same pure-key-swap fixture under requireOutOfBandKeys: entry 2 attacker key is embedded-only, so it fails closed. The vault key is supplied out of band, so entries 1 and 3 pass; the policy break surfaces at position 2.
Entry 2 denormalised payload rewritten while coseSign1 carries the original predicate — binding-integrity failure caught before any content evaluation.

Engine booted without a signing key: every row has signing_key_id=NULL and an all-zero signature slot. Chain integrity holds via hash links; the signature check is skipped (no key), never folded into a cryptographically-signed verdict.
